overview: The Gemara discusses cases where the lost item is transferred to another creditor or used for marriage, and when a creditor may consume the fruits of a seized property.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara discusses cases where the lost item is transferred to another creditor or used for marriage, and when a creditor may consume the fruits of a seized property.

steps.title: Dispute: Rav Acha and Ravina on voluntary cession
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces a dispute between Rav Acha and Ravina about whether property voluntarily ceded by a debtor must be returned if the debtor later produces money.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a dispute between Rav Acha and Ravina regarding a debtor who voluntarily cedes property to repay a debt. The question is whether the debtor must return the property if he later produces money to repay the debt.
steps.deeperAnalysis: One holds that the property must be returned (הָדְרָה), and the other holds that it need not be returned (לָא הָדְרָה). This dispute centers on the nature of voluntary cession and whether it constitutes a final transfer.

keyTerms.meaning: he ceded it for his debt
Rav Acha and Ravina disagree about it
one said: it must be returned
and one said: it must not be returned

steps.whatToRemember: The dispute centers on the nature of voluntary cession and whether it constitutes a final transfer.
steps.confusionAlert: This is not about forced seizure, but about voluntary cession by the debtor.
steps.whyThisMatters: This dispute affects the rights of debtors and creditors in cases of voluntary property transfer.

phrases.aramaic: אַגְבְּיֵהּ אִיהוּ בְּחוֹבוֹ, פְּלִיגִי בַּהּ רַב אַחָא וְרָבִינָא.
phrases.english: About his debt, Rav Acha and Ravina disagree.

phrases.aramaic: חַד אָמַר: הָדְרָה, וְחַד אָמַר: לָא הָדְרָה.
phrases.english: One says: It is returned, and one says: It is not returned.

steps.triggerLanguage: אַגְבֵּיהּ אִיהוּ בְּחוֹבוֹ, פְּלִיגִי בַּהּ רַב אַחָא וְרַבִּינָא. חַד אָמַר: הָדְרָה, וְחַד אָמַר: לָא הָדְרָה.

rashi.hebrew: אגביה איהו בחובו - לעיל מיהדר גבי שומא כלומר אם לא שמוה ב"ד למלוה על כרחו של לוה וקם ליה מעצמו ולא הטריחו לדין ואמר לו טול קרקע זה בחובך:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the case of אגביה אייהו בחובו refers to a voluntary cession where the debtor, without being forced by the court, offers property to the creditor to settle the debt.

steps.title: Explanation of the dispute: fine sale vs. embarrassment
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains the dispute: one holds that voluntary cession is a fine sale, while the other holds it is due to embarrassment.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara explains the reasoning behind the dispute between Rav Acha and Ravina.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The one who holds that the property must not be returned (לָא הָדְרָה) reasons that this is a fine sale (זְבִינֵי מְעַלְיָא), as the debtor ceded it of his own volition (מִדַּעְתָּא דְּנַפְשֵׁיהּ). The one who holds that it must be returned (הָדְרָה) reasons that this is not a fine sale, and the debtor ceded it only due to embarrassment (מֵחֲמַת כִּיסּוּפָא).

keyTerms.meaning: the one who said it must not be returned holds
for he ceded it of his own volition
and the one who said it must be returned holds
it is not a fine sale

keyTerms.term: וְהַאי דְּאַגְבֵּיהּ מִדַּעְתֵּיהּ וְלָא אֲתָא לְדִינָא
keyTerms.meaning: and that which he ceded it of his own volition and did not come to court
it was due to embarrassment that he ceded it

steps.whatToRemember: The distinction between a fine sale and a sale due to embarrassment is key to understanding the dispute.
steps.confusionAlert: This is not about the validity of the sale, but about the debtor's motivation.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction affects whether the debtor can reclaim the property if he later produces money.

phrases.english: The one who said it is not returned holds:
for he collected it of his own mind.
And the one who said it is returned holds:
It is not a superior sale,

phrases.aramaic: וְהַאי דְּאַגְבְּיֵהּ מִדַּעְתֵּיהּ וְלָא אֲתָא לְדִינָא –
phrases.english: and that which he collected it of his mind and did not come to judgment –
it is because of embarrassment that he collected it.

steps.triggerLanguage: מַאן דְּאָמַר לָא הָדְרָה סָבַר: הַאי זְבִינֵי מְעַלְיָא הִיא, דְּהָא מִדַּעְתָּא דְּנַפְשֵׁיהּ אַגְבֵּיהּ. וּמַאן דְּאָמַר הָדְרָה סָבַר: לָא זְבִינֵי מְעַלְיָא הוּא, וְהַאי דְּאַגְבֵּיהּ מִדַּעְתֵּיהּ וְלָא אֲתָא לְדִינָא – מֵחֲמַת כִּיסּוּפָא הוּא דְּאַגְבֵּיהּ.

steps.title: When can the creditor eat the fruits?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks: from when can the creditor eat the fruits of the seized property?
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara shifts to a new question about the creditor's rights to the fruits of seized property.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara introduces a new question: from when can the creditor eat the fruits (פֵּירֵי) of the seized property? This question arises because the creditor's rights to the property are not immediate, and the timing of when he can benefit from the property is a matter of halachic debate.

keyTerms.meaning: and from when can he eat the fruits?

steps.whatToRemember: The question of when the creditor can eat the fruits is a key issue in the discussion of creditor rights.
steps.confusionAlert: This is not about the creditor's rights to the property itself, but specifically to the fruits.
steps.whyThisMatters: This question affects the timing of the creditor's benefits from the seized property.

phrases.english: And from when does he eat fruits?

phrases.aramaic: רַבָּה אָמַר: מִכִּי מָטְיָא אַדְרַכְתָּא לִידֵיהּ.
phrases.english: Rabbah said: From when the document of expropriation comes to his hand.

phrases.aramaic: אַבָּיֵי אָמַר: עֵדָיו בַּחֲתוּמָיו זָכִין לוֹ.
phrases.english: Abbaye said: His witnesses acquire for him with their signing.

rashi.hebrew: מאימתי אוכל פירות - מי ששמו לו ב"ד קרקע בחובו מאימת היא קנויה לו לאכול פירות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the question of when the creditor may eat the פירות refers to a case where the בית דין has already assigned the debtor's land to the creditor as security for the debt.

rashi.hebrew: אדרכתא - לאחר תשעים יום שנפסק הדין דאמרינן בפ"ק (דף טו) דב"ד כותבין אדרכתא שטר פסק דין אנכסי לוה שבכל מקום שימצאם משלו יקחם ומוסרין לו השטר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבָּה's position, that the creditor may eat the פירות from when the אדרכתא reaches his hand, refers to the period after the ninety-day waiting period following the court's decision, during which the בית דין writes the אדרכתא (a document of judgment) and hands it over to the creditor.

rashi.hebrew: עדיו בחתומיו - מיום שנחתם שטר האדרכתא בב"ד אע"פ שלא בא לידו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אַבָּיֵי's position, that the witnesses' signatures on the document suffice, means the creditor acquires the right to the פירות from the day the אדרכתא is signed in the בית דין, even before the document physically comes into the creditor's possession.

steps.title: Rava: from when the days of announcement are completed
steps.stepSummary: Rava holds that the creditor can eat the fruits from when the days of announcement are completed.
steps.whatsHappening: Rava provides his answer to the question about when the creditor can eat the fruits.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rava answers that the creditor can eat the fruits from when the days of announcement (יְמֵי אַכְרַזְתָּא) are completed. This means that the creditor's rights to the fruits begin after the required period of public announcement has passed, ensuring that all potential claimants have had the opportunity to come forward.

keyTerms.meaning: from when the days of announcement are completed

steps.whatToRemember: Rava's opinion ties the creditor's rights to the completion of the announcement period.
steps.confusionAlert: This is not about the creditor's general rights, but specifically about the timing of his rights to the fruits.
steps.whyThisMatters: This opinion establishes a third point in time when the creditor's rights to the fruits begin.

phrases.aramaic: רָבָא אָמַר: מִכִּי שְׁלִימוּ יְמֵי אַכְרַזְתָּא.
phrases.english: Rava said: When the days of the announcement are completed.

steps.triggerLanguage: רָבָא אָמַר: מִכִּי שְׁלִימוּ יְמֵי אַכְרַזְתָּא.

rashi.hebrew: מכי שלמו ימי אכרזתא - אע"פ שבאה אדרכתא לידו ולא מצא נכסים ללוה עד לאחר זמן וכשמצא הוזקק לבא לב"ד ומכריזין שיש כאן קרקע למכור כדאמרינן בערכין (דף כא:) ואם בא זה וקיבלה ביותר ממה ששמוה אחרים מוסרין אותה בידו לאחר שכלו ימי הכרזה ובמסכת ערכין מפרש כמה ימים מכריזין בפ' שום היתומים (שם) ועד דשלמו הנך יומי הוי פירי דלוה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבא's ruling means the creditor's right to the fruits begins only after the required days of public announcement (ימי אכרזתא) have passed, even if the creditor found the property later, because until then the fruits belong to the debtor.

tosafot.hebrew: רבא אמר מכי שלמו ימי אכרזתא - וא"ת שלהי הנושא (כתובות דף קד: ושם ד"ה ה"ג) דאמר רבא אחוי אדרכתיך והתם רבא גרסינן מדאמר והא מר הוא דאמר אחריות טעות סופר הוא והיינו רבא דפסיק כן לעיל בפ"ק (בבא מציעא דף טו: ושם) וי"ל אחוי לי אדרכתיך דאי שפיר כתיבא הוה גבי מיומא דאכרזתא ואית דגרסי בשמעתין רבא אמר מכי מטא אדרכתא לידיה ובתר הכי רבה ולפי זה אתי טפי בפשיטות ההיא דהנושא והקדים רבא לאביי ורבה משום דאדרכתא קודם לאכרזתא ודברי אביי דאמר עדיו בחתומיו זכין לו מיושב טפי לשנות בתר דברי רבא מקודם:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses a contradiction with רבא's statement in כתובות regarding when the creditor's rights begin, suggesting either a different reading (מכי מטא אדרכתא לידיה) or that the order of opinions is arranged because the arrival of the document precedes the announcement period.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות attempts to resolve a contradiction between רבא's statement here and his ruling in כתובות by proposing alternative readings or explaining the logical sequence of the opinions, whereas רש״י focuses on the practical implication of the announcement period for ownership of the fruits.
